statement.p: A river flows at 7 miles per hour. A boat traveled with the current from Town A to Town B, which are 150 miles apart. The boat stayed overnight at Town B. The next day, the waters current stopped, and boat traveled on still water to reach Town C, which is 170 miles away from Town B. The second leg of the trip (Town B to Town C) took 3 hours longer than the first leg (Town A to Town B). During this whole trip, the boat was driving at a constant still-water speed. Find this speed.
Note that you should not consider the unreasonable answer.
During this trip, the boats speed on still water was miles per hour.

solution.p: Assume that the boats still-water speed was x miles per hour.

Since the currents speed was 7 miles per hour when the boat traveled from Town A to Town B (with the current), the boats actual speed was x+7 miles per hour. When the boat traveled from Town B to Town C (on still water), its actual speed was simply x miles per hour.
Using the formula time=\frac{distance}{speed}, we know the boat traveled from Town A to Town B in \frac{150}{x+7} hours; and it traveled from Town B to Town C in \frac{170}{x} hours.
Its given that the second leg of the trip took 3 hours longer, so we can write an equation and solve it:
When numbers are big, we use the quadratic formula to solve the quadratic equation:
The negative solution, -20.07 does not make sense as a boat speed in context, so we dont consider this solution. The boats still-water speed was 19.75 miles per hour.
